message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: deep-merge support in Features.update(). I only need to touch features.py. What file(s) does your feature need?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding on dict mutations.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py (same class Features). Proposal to avoid clobbering: I add a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator plus apply it as a one-line @decorator annotation above __setitem__, __delitem__, and update (no change to method bodies except __setitem__/__delitem__ which I'm implementing fresh). You keep full ownership of the internal merge logic inside update()'s body - I will only touch update()'s signature line to add the decorator, not its body. I'll implement first and send DONE with exact line numbers touched, then you layer your deep-merge body changes on top. Sound OK, or do you want to go first instead?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We're on the same file. My change is scoped to the body of Features.update() (lines ~1248-1256) to add deep-merge logic for dict values, still calling self[key]=value via __setitem__ for actual assignment. Does your keep_features_dicts_synced decorator touch update() itself, or just __setitem__/__delitem__/pop/setdefault/etc? If you leave update()'s body alone (just decorate other methods, or wrap update() only at the signature level without altering its internal loop), we should be conflict-free. Let me know which methods you'll touch so we don't clobber each other's edits to the same lines.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed: you implement first (decorator + __setitem__/__delitem__ bodies + decorator line above update()'s signature only). I'll layer my deep-merge logic inside update()'s body afterward, once I get your DONE with exact line numbers touched. Go ahead.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Confirmed: update() (lines ~1248-1256) already syncs via self[key]=value, which goes through __setitem__ - so I will NOT touch update()'s signature or body at all, that's fully yours. I'll only touch: __init__ (minor), __setitem__ (~1240-1242), __delitem__ (~1244-1246), and add NEW methods (pop, setdefault, popitem, clear, __or__/__ior__ if present) elsewhere in the class body that currently bypass sync entirely. I'll add my decorator function above the Features class. Will send DONE with exact line ranges once finished so you can layer your update() body edit on top safely.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Done. Added keep_features_dicts_synced decorator function at lines 1201-1220 (right before class Features, imports functools.wraps at line 23). Decorated __setitem__ (1262-1264), __delitem__ (1266-1268) - bodies simplified to just call super(), removed manual sync lines. Added ONE decorator line above update()'s existing signature at 1270-1271 (def update(self, iterable, **kwds): now at line 1271) - I did NOT touch its body (lines 1272-1279 unchanged from original, still self[key]=value style) so your deep-merge logic is free to modify that body. Also added new decorated methods pop/setdefault/popitem/clear at lines 1281-1295 (these previously didn't exist and bypassed sync entirely - not touching anything you own). Ran full features test suite, 25 passed. Go ahead and layer your update() body changes on lines 1272-1279.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Ack. FYI: my local working tree doesn't show your edits (we appear to be in separate environments/copies of the repo) - still clean at commit e229fade6. That's fine since we each submit our own patch.txt independently; I'll proceed to implement deep-merge only inside update()'s body per our agreed split, leaving __setitem__/__delitem__/pop/setdefault/etc and the decorator to you. Proceeding now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented deep-merge in Features.update() body only (lines ~1248-1263). Handles: nested dict merge at arbitrary depth, Sequence-wrapped dict merge (current.feature), plain overwrite otherwise, works for both iterable and **kwds. Ran full features test suite: 25 passed, 3 skipped. Did not touch __init__/__setitem__/__delitem__/pop/setdefault/etc. Patch written to patch.txt. All good on my end.
